does: This paper develops and applies a rigorous candidate evaluation protocol for hidden-state probes designed to detect indirect prompt injection attacks in multimodal vision-language agents. Rather than accepting high AUC scores at face value, the authors conduct a detailed case study on Qwen2.5-VL-7B using the Mind2Web benchmark under teacher-forced replay conditions. They introduce two post-hoc diagnostic tools: a paired-construction scalar baseline for text-side injections that isolates the contribution of textual attack content, and same-step nuisance-matched visual controls that test whether probes genuinely detect malicious semantics or merely respond to surface-level features like overlay presence. The paper demonstrates that a clean-versus-attack AUC of 0.998 does not necessarily indicate robust malicious-content detection, and proposes concrete heuristics for interpreting what high AUC scores actually validate.
connects: This work builds on the emerging body of research into hidden-state probing as a mechanistic evaluation tool for neural networks, extending methods initially developed for understanding language model behavior to the multimodal domain. It responds directly to recent work on indirect prompt injection vulnerabilities in computer-use agents and the appeal of linear probes on frozen model activations as lightweight detection mechanisms. The paper engages with broader concerns about evaluating adversarial robustness in vision-language models and relates to research on bias and spurious correlations in neural network classifiers. The diagnostic approach echoes methodological rigor from interpretability research that questions whether high performance metrics reflect genuine semantic understanding or surface-level pattern matching, connecting to work on shortcut learning and adversarial examples.
enables: This protocol establishes a replicable framework for more cautious evaluation of hidden-state probes in adversarial settings, raising the bar for what constitutes credible malicious-content detection. The proposed diagnostics and reporting heuristics enable future work to move beyond headline metrics toward mechanistic understanding of what probes actually capture. The findings suggest several directions: developing probes that are invariant to injection-surface artifacts, designing more comprehensive control sets across different attack modalities and vision-language architectures, and investigating whether probe performance generalizes across different backbones and benchmarks beyond the studied case. The work also points toward better evaluation practices for adversarial robustness in multimodal agents more broadly, potentially informing how to build more trustworthy safety evaluation protocols for deployed systems.
